**Machine:** Generation X (Gen-X w/ Double Roller vacuum feeder & spool feeder)
**Subsystem:** Linear - Length Positioning
**Component:** Drive
**Approximate date:** 2026-02

## Symptoms
Linear motor was moving normally during operation. An error will show on the operator screen stating a Kill Command was issued or a fault for the linear was issued.

## Troubleshooting steps
1. Check LED on Compax3 drive, Check Error History, & try to reset fault status using the QT button on the Optimization screen in Compax Servo Manager _o_change]_
2. Check there is no obstruction to linear movement. Check stainless cover is not rubbing, cable is pulling, or interference from other tools on the machine. _o_change]_
3. Remove the X3 connector on C3 drive and check resistance across U, V, W, ground, & shield to see if there is damage to the motor. _o_change]_
4. Replaced Compax3 drive with compatible E-2012-5 drive. _ound_it]_

## Root cause
Compax3 E-2012-6 was defective.

## Resolution
We confirmed the mechanics and the cable/motor health was good. That left a defective drive that most likely had internal parts that were failing. We replaced the E-2012-6 drive with a compatible E-2012-5 drive we had in stock and the position error stopped occurring.

## Parts involved
- `E-2012-5`

**Machine:** Generation X (Gen-X3 w/ long wire feeder, 4th axis for dental files)
**Subsystem:** Ram - Diameter Positioning
**Component:** Ram Motor & Glass Scale
**Approximate date:** 2026-02

## Symptoms
When either the operator jogs the ram or runs a cycle, the ACR will show a Kill command and a drive fault for the ram Compax3. Checking the error history of the Compax3, it shows error 7320 Position Difference.

## Troubleshooting steps
1. Check cables and clean glass scale. _o_change]_
2. Check mechanics of ballscrew for any loose items such as ballscrew bearings, coupling of ballscrew and coupling of gearhead. Check motor shaft is smooth to turn. _o_change]_
3. Check winding resistance of Ram motor. _o_change]_
4. Tested Ram with Linear Compax3 _o_change]_
5. Disabled Glass Scale function on Compax3. _mproved]_
6. Replaced glass scale. _ound_it]_

## Root cause
Most likely read head electronics failed due to amount of coolant present.

## Resolution
After all the items above were done, we learned the replacement glass scale may have been used or repaired. The current glass scale was disabled on the Compax3 and the customer was able to run the machine for production temporarily. A new scale was sent and mounted. The glass scale was re-enabled on the Compax3 drive and the machine is back in production.

## Parts involved
- `E-2032-1`"},{"id":"a9754f1b-46d6-5a1e-a892-6f82cc91221a","modality":"text","matched_by":["text"],"score":1.0609,"components":{"vector":0.6726,"sparse":null,"rerank":1.0609},"source_id":"hc_69d51a9f71607d91eaebd1ac","source_type":"historical_case","title":null,"page":null,"sticky":"corroborated","text":"# Linear Drive Fault

**Machine:** Generation X
**Subsystem:** Linear - Length Positioning
**Component:** E-1892D
**Approximate date:** 2026-02

## Symptoms
Trying to enable the linear motor either through the PLC or manually in the Axis Status Screen causes a drive fault/kill.

## Troubleshooting steps
1. Check Error History of Compax3 drive in Optimization Screen and tried to clear using the QT button on bottom left. _o_change]_
2. Disabled ACR drive fault response. _o_change]_
3. Exchanged Signal Cables from ACR to Collet/GV and ACR to Linear/GV _o_change]_
4. A technician opened signal cable and repaired wires that were damaged. _ound_it]_

## Root cause
The signal cable from the ACR to the GV servo drive was damaged.

## Resolution
Customer removed the suspect cable and opened the covers for the connectors. They found some wires had come off their solder points and repaired the cable. Once installed, the system worked normally.

## Parts involved
- `E-1892D`"},{"id":"99898f4c-83e1-5a92-a3e1-11bd4cce4581","modality":"visual","matched_by":["visual"],"score":0.8971,"components":{"vector":22.7697,"sparse":null,"rerank":0.8971},"source_id":"doc_a169a18398e84584b0b2eeca","source_type":"manual","title":"Parker Compax3 Manual","page":130,"thumbnail_gcs_key":"pages/royal-master/doc_a169a18398e84584b0b2eeca/page-0130.png"},{"id":"98bd38c3-a1ea-52fb-bcb0-102420d136dc","modality":"visual","matched_by":["visual"],"score":0.8841,"components":{"vector":22.7456,"sparse":null,"rerank":0.8841},"source_id":"doc_a169a18398e84584b0b2eeca","source_type":"manual","title":"Parker Compax3 Manual","page":32,"thumbnail_gcs_key":"pages/royal-master/doc_a169a18398e84584b0b2eeca/page-0032.png"},{"id":"e612ac93-8c18-5ba3-a490-6ad98787c851","modality":"text","matched_by":["text"],"score":0.8756,"components":{"vector":0.68,"sparse":null,"rerank":0.8756},"source_id":"hc_69dd48d222da05c6d023d1bf","source_type":"historical_case","title":null,"page":null,"text":"# Kill all motion for Ram

**Subsystem:** Ram Axis
**Component:** Ram Drive
**Approximate date:** 2026-03

## Symptoms
Randomly the screen will report a "Kill Motion Command for Ram" and "The Ram Drive Axis has Faulted".

## Troubleshooting steps
1. Log into C3 drive and find error history. Try to clear error with QT button on bottom left of Optimization screen in Compax Servo Manager _o_change]_
2. with I2T error, check motor windings for resistance to detect any damage. _o_change]_
3. Check Mechancis of ram is in good working order. _o_change]_
4. Replace Ram Compax3 Drive _ound_it]_

## Resolution
Replacement Drive was sent and installed. Errors are gone.

**Machine:** Generation X (GenX3)
**Subsystem:** CNC Dresser Back of the Wheel
**Component:** X Axis
**Approximate date:** 2026-04

## Symptoms
When operator turns on the X Axis, it faults and will not enable.

## Troubleshooting steps
1. Remove U, V, W wires from front of drive and measure resistance of motor phases to each other, to ground, and to shield. _o_change]_
2. Remove U, V, W wires from front of drive and try to enable drive. _o_change]_
3. Replace Drive with new Compax3 drive. _o_change]_

## Measurements
U to V 4.1
V to W 4.1
W to U 4.2
U,V,W to ground OL
U,V,W to shield OL

## Root cause
Aries Drive failed, Bridge Circuit not working.

## Resolution
Replaced Aries drive with newer Compax3 drive.

## Parts involved

- `E-2012-5`"},{"id":"b10b2816-b946-5693-a404-b56ae410cf09","modality":"visual","matched_by":["visual"],"score":0.8626,"components":{"vector":0.8626,"sparse":null,"rerank":0.8626},"source_id":"doc_c6104a26cffc423bb6eea1cf","source_type":"drawing","title":"Electrical","page":13,"sticky":"injected","thumbnail_gcs_key":"pages/royal-master/doc_c6104a26cffc423bb6eea1cf/page-0013.png"},{"id":"bbc90b74-03fb-5dac-99a8-57abe3ba5439","modality":"visual","matched_by":["visual"],"score":0.7654,"components":{"vector":23.0621,"sparse":null,"rerank":0.7654},"source_id":"doc_a169a18398e84584b0b2eeca","source_type":"manual","title":"Parker Compax3 Manual","page":85,"thumbnail_gcs_key":"pages/royal-master/doc_a169a18398e84584b0b2eeca/page-0085.png"},{"id":"bdbfa2ff-1842-5094-8b28-e29c104f0bcc","modality":"visual","matched_by":["visual"],"score":0.7542,"components":{"vector":22.9915,"sparse":null,"rerank":0.7542},"source_id":"doc_a169a18398e84584b0b2eeca","source_type":"manual","title":"Parker Compax3 Manual","page":28,"thumbnail_gcs_key":"pages/royal-master/doc_a169a18398e84584b0b2eeca/page-0028.png"},{"id":"35c9d8a8-8495-57fd-b6d4-aa52f534e2b2","modality":"text","matched_by":["text"],"score":0.7394,"components":{"vector":0.6795,"sparse":null,"rerank":0.7394},"source_id":"hc_69dd3f6722da05c6d023d1be","source_type":"historical_case","title":null,"page":null,"text":"# Kill all motion for Ram

**Machine:** Generation X (GenX Single Roller Vacuum w/ Exit Side Unloader)
**Subsystem:** CNC Dresser Back of the Wheel
**Component:** Z Axis
**Approximate date:** 2026-04

## Symptoms
Drive will disable and throw a fault output to the ACR

## Troubleshooting steps
1. Check Resistance across U, V, W, Ground & Shield of the motor. _o_change]_
2. Check Encoder interpolator box for error. _o_change]_
3. Remove Encoder cable cover to check for loose or broken wires. _o_change]_
4. Change drive to E-2012-5 Compax3 drive. _ound_it]_

## Measurements
4.8 ohms across U, V, and W. No Connection to ground or shield.

## Root cause
Aries drive has failed. Procured by 3rd party vendor and cannot confirm if it was repaired.

## Resolution
We took out the suspect Aries drive and replaced it with a Compax3 drive. In addition to the drive, we replaced the ACR signal cable to the drive with an E-2012J and used an adaptor E-2012I to convert the Aries feedback cable to be compatible with the Compax3 drive.

## Parts involved
- `E-2012-5`
- `E-2012I`
- `E-2012J`"},{"id":"d66eaac5-f0ec-58a2-9877-fb2550528c86","modality":"text","matched_by":["text"],"score":0.7168,"components":{"vector":0.6923,"sparse":null,"rerank":0.7168},"source_id":"hc_69de402c22da05c6d023d1c1","source_type":"historical_case","title":null,"page":null,"text":"# Machine startup issue related to CNC dresser back of the wheel

**Machine:** Generation X
**Subsystem:** CNC Dresser Back of the Wheel
**Component:** X Axis, Z Axis
**Approximate date:** 2026-04

## Symptoms
Can't get X or Z axes moving

## Root cause
Incorrect .mgf file was downloaded to drives. File downloaded was for the LC416 encoder only, but these axes need the .mgf file that contains the motor information as well as the LC416 encoder information.

## Resolution
Correct .mgf file was downloaded and polarity detection was performed. Lower axis started up just fine, but polarity detection parameters needed to be tweaked to get upper axis to work. Changed polarity detection load level from 200 to 500, and changed polarity detection confirmation torque back to default."},{"id":"1996425b-1730-5a82-a7f0-e442e3a6882d","modality":"text","matched_by":["text"],"score":0.7095,"components":{"vector":0.6923,"sparse":null,"rerank":0.7095},"source_id":"hc_69de402a22da05c6d023d1c0","source_type":"historical_case","title":null,"page":null,"sticky":"corroborated","text":"# Machine startup issue related to CNC dresser back of the wheel

The motor is controlled by the servo drive with control voltage U. During motion of the motor, an internal back e.m.f. U<sub>EMK</sub> is induced. This antagonizes the control voltage and is therefore deduced in the motor model. The difference is available for the acceleration of the motor.

The first order delay component represents the delaying property of the motor winding with the time constant T=L/R. According to Ohm's Law, a current I=U/R results.

The drive torque of the motor is calculated by multiplying the current with the motor torque constant K<sub>T</sub>. This is antagonized by the load torque of the machine.

The remaining acceleration torque accelerates the motor.

The resulting acceleration depends on the total mass moment of inertia (= motor + load moment of inertia).

The integration of the acceleration (sum of the acceleration over time) results in the velocity of the motor, which influences the amplitude of the induced EMC voltage.","thumbnail_gcs_key":"pages/royal-master/doc_a169a18398e84584b0b2eeca/page-0172.png"},{"point_id":"1ed05562-8bea-571a-838a-5a3314a6ed98","source_id":"doc_98ada30a38224702874f8e02","source_type":"manual","title":"Gen-X Manual","page_number":26,"chunk_index":25,"start_time_s":null,"end_time_s":null,"vector_score":0.0135,"gcs_key":null,"text":"### 2.4.2 Standard Machine TG-12x4

#### *INSTALL THE AIR CYLINDER*

Use the following sequence to install the air cylinder.

1. Make sure the collet closer until is locked on the pin that you used at the end of operation #2.
2. The cylinder shaft should be in the full retract position with NO air attached.
3. Install the 1226-00 eye bolt into the end of the piston shaft to the length required for the eye bolt to fit over the stud, and the cylinder to fit against the spacers without binding the closer unit.
4. Connect the air and test the cycle to see if the closer "clicks" locked and the shoes are not binding after the air dumps. Rotate the eye bolt in or out until you achieve the proper location. Adjust the time delay relay if necessary to allow the air to stay on long enough for the collet to lock before it dumps the exhaust air.
5. Test run the spindle at various speeds for a few minutes to verify proper installation. The spindle should not heat up if everything is properly set.

DO NOT CYCLE WITH SPINDLE RUNNING.**","thumbnail_gcs_key":"pages/royal-master/doc_98ada30a38224702874f8e02/page-0050.png"},{"point_id":"eb544fa0-af01-52dd-9171-2d927277e783","source_id":"doc_a169a18398e84584b0b2eeca","source_type":"manual","title":"Parker Compax3 Manual","page_number":171,"chunk_index":196,"start_time_s":null,"end_time_s":null,"vector_score":0.013,"gcs_key":null,"text":"### Overview of the processes during configuration and setup of the Compax3 drive system

The controller presettings are calculated from the configured motor and application parameters with the aid of the automatic controller design which runs in the background.

These controller presettings provide normally for a stable and robust control. Due to continually rising application requirements, this presetting is often not sufficient, so that further optimization of the control behavior is necessary.

This manual describes the setup and optimization procedure for Compax3.

In order to better understand the correlations and interactions, we will describe in the first step the individual correlations and physical values, that are required for the configuration and the prespecification of the control loops. In the following, the manual will then describe the function blocks for the optimization implemented in the servo drive as well as the setup tool.

### **Software for supporting the configuration, setup and optimization**

!creenshot of the C3 ServoManager2 software interface showing the Compax3 drive system configuration. The interface includes a 3D model of the drive unit and various parameter settings.](f320a32746dfa353ad8363c93bd53dd5_img.jpg)

The entry of the motor and application parameters is made with the C3 ServoManager2 (C3Mgr2.exe):

The configuration requires:

### Application parameters

The wizard guided entry of the application parameters takes place directly in the ServoManager.

**Carefully verify the entries and default values in order to detect entry errors in the run-up.**

After the configuration download, the drive can be set up and be optimized if needs be. For this, please open the optimization window of the ServoManager:

!creenshot of the ServoManager software interface showing the optimization window. The window displays a graph of motor performance (speed, torque, current) over time, with a red line indicating the target values and a blue line indicating the actual values. The introduced terms:

- ♦ Stability
- ♦ Damping
- ♦ Velocity
- ♦ Bandwidth
- ♦ Setpoint and disturbance behavior
- ♦ Control variable limitation
- ♦ Replacement time constant
- ♦ Rigidity

are related as follows:

- ♦ A well-attenuated control features a stable control behavior.
- ♦ The velocity of a control loop is a measure for the reaction rate of the controller to the disturbance variable (disturbance behavior) as well as to the setpoint variable (setpoint behavior).
- ♦ The faster the control, the higher its bandwidth.
- ♦ The term replacement time constant is an approximation and is only valid in a defined scope1. In this scope, the control is always stable and well-attenuated.
- ♦ If the controller does not work in the linear range, but the control variable of the controller is within the limitation, the control slows down and the control difference rises.
- ♦ The stiffness represents the bandwidth of the velocity control. The higher the stiffness value of the velocity control, the higher the bandwidth of the velocity controller and the stiffer the drive.

### **Automated controller design**

#### In this chapter you can read about:

Step response of the velocity loop depending on the optimization parameter "attenuation" and "stiffness"..... 198

The controller design takes place after the configuration immediately before the configuration download into the device. The controller coefficients are preassigned according to the design method of cross-ratios so that a stable control is achieved. The automatic, robust controller design calculates the P and I terms of the individual controllers (current, velocity, position) on the basis of the configured motor and application parameters.

**Please observe:** Faulty motor and application parameters may lead under certain circumstances to instable controllers.

The controller parameters are not directly available for the optimization. Instead, they can be changed with the aid of the following optimization parameters:

- |                                                  |                                      |
|--------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Optimization of the current controller dynamics: | ♦ Current loop bandwidth in %        |
| Optimization of the velocity loop dynamics:      | ♦ "Attenuation of current loop" in % |
|                                                  | ♦ "Stiffness" in %                   |
|                                                  | ♦ "Attenuation" in %                 |
|                                                  | ♦ Velocity loop - "D" term in %      |

The bandwidth parameter states the actually effective % of the calculated default velocity. The default bandwidth of the current controller is fixed to approx. fGR=531Hz. In reverse this signifies that each motor delivers the same step response. The prerequisite is, of course, that you keep out of the control signal limitation (voltage limitation). The attenuation characterizes the controller's tendency to oscillate with respect to an excitation signal (see below). The stiffness (of the velocity loop, corresponds to the bandwidth of the current loop) describes the velocity of the velocity loop (see below).","thumbnail_gcs_key":"pages/royal-master/doc_a169a18398e84584b0b2eeca/page-0197.png"},{"point_id":"ae41b24f-71dc-5e08-ba03-a1ce7df94667","source_id":"doc_a169a18398e84584b0b2eeca","source_type":"manual","title":"Parker Compax3 Manual","page_number":78,"chunk_index":90,"start_time_s":null,"end_time_s":null,"vector_score":0.0129,"gcs_key":null,"text":"## 3.9.3.3 Mounting distances, air currents Compax3H1xxV4

A film of clean grease must be maintained on the collet closer shoes (See 3.1.7). A film of oil should be maintained on rear end of headstock spindle and on the collet seat of spindle to prevent sticking and provide smooth operation. Usually a single drop of light spindle oil will be adequate for this purpose (see 3.1.8).

### **3.3.2 OVERHEATING OF CAM**

In the event the collet closer cam becomes hot to the touch, it is an indication that the shoes are riding against the slot in the cam. This condition is most easily corrected by removing the nylon shoes and filing them down enough to allow a few thousandths of play in the slot when the collet closer is in the locked position. Shoe part number is X-665-2.

2026-05-21 18:41:22,424 INFO  rm.slog: {"ts":"2026-05-21T18:41:22.424Z","service":"portal-backend","event":"trace.phase.end","trace_id":"conv_6a0f4e53b255280608e513ca","phase":"generate","started_at_ms":47597,"duration_ms":13396,"meta":{"model":"gemini-3-flash-preview","usage":{"input_tokens":19933,"output_tokens":656},"finish_reason":"stop","answer_chars":2390,"answer_text":"The fact that the error returns after only two parts, even after a logic reset and an X4 power cycle, indicates a persistent hardware or signal integrity issue rather than a simple software glitch. 

The **X4 connector** you pulled provides the 24VDC control voltage and enable signals rc:a29abcde]. While this resets the drive's logic and clears electronic fuses rc:761948bc], it cannot fix physical damage.

Based on your observations and the machine's history, there are three primary suspects:

1.  **The Compax3 Drive (E-2012-5 or E-2012-6):** As you noted with machine G-39, these drives can fail internally, leading to intermittent following errors rc:6dafa87a]. The "device type does not match" warning in your screenshot suggests the drive may be struggling to maintain its configuration or is communicating incorrectly with the software rc:c60c4469].
2.  **The Signal Cable (E-1892D):** The "wires" you found inside the motor cover are a major red flag. If those were strands from the signal cable, the intermittent loss of even one wire will cause a **7320 Following error** or the **8130 FBI Timeout** you saw earlier rc:7b8707d8].
3.  **The Glass Scale / Read Head (E-2032-1 / E-3073):** Oil contamination inside the motor housing often leads to the failure of the read head electronics rc:f6ab94da]. If the read head cannot accurately report the motor's position back to the drive, the drive will issue a Kill command rc:f6ab94da].

**Next Step:**
Since you have already confirmed the motor windings are healthy (~10.7 Ω) rc:6dafa87a], and the issue is identical to a previous drive failure on G-39, replacing the drive is a logical next step. However, I strongly recommend replacing the signal cable at the same time, given the physical debris you found.

Would you like me to open a support ticket to get a replacement drive and signal cable sent out?
